1.text: The song<S>7892</S> of songs<S>7892</S>, which is Solomon's<S>8010</S>.
Let him kiss<S>5401</S> me with the kisses<S>5390</S> of his mouth<S>6310</S>: for thy love<S>1730</S> is better<S>2896</S> than wine<S>3196</S>. <sup>thy: Heb. thy loves</sup>

Because of the savour<S>7381</S> of thy good<S>2896</S> ointments<S>8081</S> thy name<S>8034</S> is as ointment<S>8081</S> poured forth<S>7324</S>, therefore do the virgins<S>5959</S> love<S>157</S> thee.

Draw<S>4900</S> me, we will run<S>7323</S> after<S>310</S> thee: the king<S>4428</S> hath brought<S>935</S> me into his chambers<S>2315</S>: we will be glad<S>1523</S> and rejoice<S>8055</S> in thee, we will remember<S>2142</S> thy love<S>1730</S> more than wine<S>3196</S>: the upright<S>4339</S> love<S>157</S> thee. <sup>the upright: or, they love thee uprightly</sup>
I am black<S>7838</S>, but comely<S>5000</S>, O ye daughters<S>1323</S> of Jerusalem<S>3389</S>, as the tents<S>168</S> of Kedar<S>6938</S>, as the curtains<S>3407</S> of Solomon<S>8010</S>.

Look<S>7200</S> not upon me, because I am black<S>7840</S>, because the sun<S>8121</S> hath looked<S>7805</S> upon me: my mother's<S>517</S> children<S>1121</S> were angry<S>2787</S> with me; they made<S>7760</S> me the keeper<S>5201</S> of the vineyards<S>3754</S>; but mine own vineyard<S>3754</S> have I not kept<S>5201</S>.

Tell<S>5046</S> me, O thou whom my soul<S>5315</S> loveth<S>157</S>, where thou feedest<S>7462</S>, where<S>349</S> thou makest thy flock to rest<S>7257</S> at noon<S>6672</S>: for<S>4100</S> why should I be as one that turneth aside<S>5844</S> by the flocks<S>5739</S> of thy companions<S>2270</S>? <sup>as one: or, as one that is veiled</sup>

If thou know<S>3045</S> not, O thou fairest<S>3303</S> among women<S>802</S>, go thy way forth<S>3318</S> by the footsteps<S>6119</S> of the flock<S>6629</S>, and feed<S>7462</S> thy kids<S>1429</S> beside the shepherds'<S>7462</S> tents<S>4908</S>.
I have compared<S>1819</S> thee, O my love<S>7474</S>, to a company of horses<S>5484</S> in Pharaoh's<S>6547</S> chariots<S>7393</S>.
Thy cheeks<S>3895</S> are comely<S>4998</S> with rows<S>8447</S> of jewels, thy neck<S>6677</S> with chains<S>2737</S> of gold.
We will make<S>6213</S> thee borders<S>8447</S> of gold<S>2091</S> with studs<S>5351</S> of silver<S>3701</S>.
While the king<S>4428</S> sitteth at his table<S>4524</S>, my spikenard<S>5373</S> sendeth forth<S>5414</S> the smell<S>7381</S> thereof.
A bundle<S>6872</S> of myrrh<S>4753</S> is my wellbeloved<S>1730</S> unto me; he shall lie<S>3885</S> all night betwixt my breasts<S>7699</S>.
My beloved<S>1730</S> is unto me as a cluster<S>811</S> of camphire<S>3724</S> in the vineyards<S>3754</S> of Engedi<S>5872</S>. <sup>camphire: or, cypress</sup>
Behold, thou art fair<S>3303</S>, my love<S>7474</S>; behold, thou art fair<S>3303</S>; thou hast doves'<S>3123</S> eyes<S>5869</S>. <sup>my love: or, my companion</sup>
Behold, thou art fair<S>3303</S>, my beloved<S>1730</S>, yea, pleasant<S>5273</S>: also our bed<S>6210</S> is green<S>7488</S>.
The beams<S>6982</S> of our house<S>1004</S> are cedar<S>730</S>, and our rafters<S>7351</S> <S>7351</S> of fir<S>1266</S>. <sup>rafters: or, galleries</sup>

2.text: I am the rose<S>2261</S> of Sharon<S>8289</S>, and the lily<S>7799</S> of the valleys<S>6010</S>.
As the lily<S>7799</S> among thorns<S>2336</S>, so is my love<S>7474</S> among the daughters<S>1323</S>.

As the apple tree<S>8598</S> among the trees<S>6086</S> of the wood<S>3293</S>, so is my beloved<S>1730</S> among the sons<S>1121</S>. I sat<S>3427</S> down under his shadow<S>6738</S> with great delight<S>2530</S>, and his fruit<S>6529</S> was sweet<S>4966</S> to my taste<S>2441</S>. <sup>I sat: Heb. I delighted and sat down, etc</sup> <sup>taste: Heb. palate</sup>
He brought<S>935</S> me to the banqueting<S>3196</S> house<S>1004</S>, and his banner<S>1714</S> over me was love<S>160</S>. <sup>banqueting: Heb. house of wine</sup>
Stay<S>5564</S> me with flagons<S>809</S>, comfort<S>7502</S> me with apples<S>8598</S>: for I am sick<S>2470</S> of love<S>160</S>. <sup>comfort: Heb. straw me with apples</sup>
His left hand<S>8040</S> is under my head<S>7218</S>, and his right hand<S>3225</S> doth embrace<S>2263</S> me.

I charge<S>7650</S> you, O ye daughters<S>1323</S> of Jerusalem<S>3389</S>, by the roes<S>6643</S>, and<S>176</S> by the hinds<S>355</S> of the field<S>7704</S>, that ye stir not up<S>5782</S>, nor awake<S>5782</S> my love<S>160</S>, till he please<S>2654</S>. <sup>I charge: Heb. I adjure you</sup>
The voice<S>6963</S> of my beloved<S>1730</S>! behold, he cometh<S>935</S> leaping<S>1801</S> upon the mountains<S>2022</S>, skipping<S>7092</S> upon the hills<S>1389</S>.

My beloved<S>1730</S> is like<S>1819</S> a roe<S>6643</S> or a young<S>6082</S> hart<S>354</S>: behold, he standeth<S>5975</S> behind<S>310</S> our wall<S>3796</S>, he looketh forth<S>7688</S> at the windows<S>2474</S>, shewing<S>6692</S> himself through the lattice<S>2762</S>. <sup>shewing: Heb. flourishing</sup>
My beloved<S>1730</S> spake<S>6030</S>, and said<S>559</S> unto me, Rise up<S>6965</S>, my love<S>7474</S>, my fair one<S>3303</S>, and come away<S>3212</S>.
For, lo, the winter<S>5638</S> is past<S>5674</S>, the rain<S>1653</S> is over<S>2498</S> and gone<S>1980</S>;

The flowers<S>5339</S> appear<S>7200</S> on the earth<S>776</S>; the time<S>6256</S> of the singing<S>2158</S> of birds is come<S>5060</S>, and the voice<S>6963</S> of the turtle<S>8449</S> is heard<S>8085</S> in our land<S>776</S>;

The fig tree<S>8384</S> putteth forth<S>2590</S> her green figs<S>6291</S>, and the vines<S>1612</S> with the tender grape<S>5563</S> give<S>5414</S> a good smell<S>7381</S>. Arise<S>6965</S>, my love<S>7474</S>, my fair one<S>3303</S>, and come away<S>3212</S>.

O my dove<S>3123</S>, that art in the clefts<S>2288</S> of the rock<S>5553</S>, in the secret<S>5643</S> places of the stairs<S>4095</S>, let me see<S>7200</S> thy countenance<S>4758</S>, let me hear<S>8085</S> thy voice<S>6963</S>; for sweet<S>6156</S> is thy voice<S>6963</S>, and thy countenance<S>4758</S> is comely<S>5000</S>.
Take<S>270</S> us the foxes<S>7776</S>, the little<S>6996</S> foxes<S>7776</S>, that spoil<S>2254</S> the vines<S>3754</S>: for our vines<S>3754</S> have tender grapes<S>5563</S>.
My beloved<S>1730</S> is mine, and I am his: he feedeth<S>7462</S> among the lilies<S>7799</S>.

Until the day<S>3117</S> break<S>6315</S>, and the shadows<S>6752</S> flee away<S>5127</S>, turn<S>5437</S>, my beloved<S>1730</S>, and be thou like<S>1819</S> a roe<S>6643</S> or a young<S>6082</S> hart<S>354</S> upon the mountains<S>2022</S> of Bether<S>1336</S>. <sup>of Bether: or, of division</sup>

3.text: By night<S>3915</S> on my bed<S>4904</S> I sought<S>1245</S> him whom my soul<S>5315</S> loveth<S>157</S>: I sought<S>1245</S> him, but I found<S>4672</S> him not.

I will rise<S>6965</S> now, and go about<S>5437</S> the city<S>5892</S> in the streets<S>7784</S>, and in the broad ways<S>7339</S> I will seek<S>1245</S> him whom my soul<S>5315</S> loveth<S>157</S>: I sought<S>1245</S> him, but I found<S>4672</S> him not.
The watchmen<S>8104</S> that go about<S>5437</S> the city<S>5892</S> found<S>4672</S> me: to whom I said, Saw<S>7200</S> ye him whom my soul<S>5315</S> loveth<S>157</S>?

It was but a little<S>4592</S> that I passed<S>5674</S> from them, but I found<S>4672</S> him whom my soul<S>5315</S> loveth<S>157</S>: I held<S>270</S> him, and would not let him go<S>7503</S>, until I had brought<S>935</S> him into my mother's<S>517</S> house<S>1004</S>, and into the chamber<S>2315</S> of her that conceived<S>2029</S> me.

I charge<S>7650</S> you, O ye daughters<S>1323</S> of Jerusalem<S>3389</S>, by the roes<S>6643</S>, and<S>176</S> by the hinds<S>355</S> of the field<S>7704</S>, that ye stir not up<S>5782</S>, nor awake<S>5782</S> my love<S>160</S>, till he please<S>2654</S>.

Who is this that cometh<S>5927</S> out of the wilderness<S>4057</S> like pillars<S>8490</S> of smoke<S>6227</S>, perfumed<S>6999</S> with myrrh<S>4753</S> and frankincense<S>3828</S>, with all powders<S>81</S> of the merchant<S>7402</S>?
Behold his bed<S>4296</S>, which is Solomon's<S>8010</S>; threescore<S>8346</S> valiant<S>1368</S> men are about<S>5439</S> it, of the valiant<S>1368</S> of Israel<S>3478</S>.

They all hold<S>270</S> swords<S>2719</S>, being expert<S>3925</S> in war<S>4421</S>: every man<S>376</S> hath his sword<S>2719</S> upon his thigh<S>3409</S> because of fear<S>6343</S> in the night<S>3915</S>.
King<S>4428</S> Solomon<S>8010</S> made<S>6213</S> himself a chariot<S>668</S> of the wood<S>6086</S> of Lebanon<S>3844</S>. <sup>a chariot: or, a bed</sup>

He made<S>6213</S> the pillars<S>5982</S> thereof of silver<S>3701</S>, the bottom<S>7507</S> thereof of gold<S>2091</S>, the covering<S>4817</S> of it of purple<S>713</S>, the midst<S>8432</S> thereof being paved<S>7528</S> with love<S>160</S>, for the daughters<S>1323</S> of Jerusalem<S>3389</S>.

Go forth<S>3318</S>, O ye daughters<S>1323</S> of Zion<S>6726</S>, and behold<S>7200</S> king<S>4428</S> Solomon<S>8010</S> with the crown<S>5850</S> wherewith his mother<S>517</S> crowned<S>5849</S> him in the day<S>3117</S> of his espousals<S>2861</S>, and in the day<S>3117</S> of the gladness<S>8057</S> of his heart<S>3820</S>.

4.text: Behold, thou art fair<S>3303</S>, my love<S>7474</S>; behold, thou art fair<S>3303</S>; thou hast doves'<S>3123</S> eyes<S>5869</S> within<S>1157</S> thy locks<S>6777</S>: thy hair<S>8181</S> is as a flock<S>5739</S> of goats<S>5795</S>, that appear<S>1570</S> from mount<S>2022</S> Gilead<S>1568</S>. <sup>that: or, that eat of, etc</sup>

Thy teeth<S>8127</S> are like a flock<S>5739</S> of sheep that are even shorn<S>7094</S>, which came up<S>5927</S> from the washing<S>7367</S>; whereof every one bear twins<S>8382</S>, and none is barren<S>7909</S> among them.

Thy lips<S>8193</S> are like a thread<S>2339</S> of scarlet<S>8144</S>, and thy speech<S>4057</S> is comely<S>5000</S>: thy temples<S>7541</S> are like a piece<S>6400</S> of a pomegranate<S>7416</S> within<S>1157</S> thy locks<S>6777</S>.

Thy neck<S>6677</S> is like the tower<S>4026</S> of David<S>1732</S> builded<S>1129</S> for an armoury<S>8530</S>, whereon there hang<S>8518</S> a thousand<S>505</S> bucklers<S>4043</S>, all shields<S>7982</S> of mighty men<S>1368</S>.
Thy two<S>8147</S> breasts<S>7699</S> are like two<S>8147</S> young<S>6082</S> roes<S>6646</S> that are twins<S>8380</S>, which feed<S>7462</S> among the lilies<S>7799</S>.

Until the day<S>3117</S> break<S>6315</S>, and the shadows<S>6752</S> flee away<S>5127</S>, I will get<S>3212</S> me to the mountain<S>2022</S> of myrrh<S>4753</S>, and to the hill<S>1389</S> of frankincense<S>3828</S>. <sup>break: Heb. breathe</sup>
Thou art all fair<S>3303</S>, my love<S>7474</S>; there is no spot<S>3971</S> in thee.

Come<S>935</S> with me from Lebanon<S>3844</S>, my spouse<S>3618</S>, with me from Lebanon<S>3844</S>: look<S>7789</S> from the top<S>7218</S> of Amana<S>549</S>, from the top<S>7218</S> of Shenir<S>8149</S> and Hermon<S>2768</S>, from the lions'<S>738</S> dens<S>4585</S>, from the mountains<S>2042</S> of the leopards<S>5246</S>.

Thou hast ravished my heart<S>3823</S>, my sister<S>269</S>, my spouse<S>3618</S>; thou hast ravished my heart<S>3823</S> with one<S>259</S> of thine eyes<S>5869</S>, with one<S>259</S> chain<S>6060</S> of thy neck<S>6677</S>. <sup>ravished: or, taken away</sup>

How fair<S>3302</S> is thy love<S>1730</S>, my sister<S>269</S>, my spouse<S>3618</S>! how much better<S>2895</S> is thy love<S>1730</S> than wine<S>3196</S>! and the smell<S>7381</S> of thine ointments<S>8081</S> than all spices<S>1314</S>!

Thy lips<S>8193</S>, O my spouse<S>3618</S>, drop<S>5197</S> as the honeycomb<S>5317</S>: honey<S>1706</S> and milk<S>2461</S> are under thy tongue<S>3956</S>; and the smell<S>7381</S> of thy garments<S>8008</S> is like the smell<S>7381</S> of Lebanon<S>3844</S>.

A garden<S>1588</S> inclosed<S>5274</S> is my sister<S>269</S>, my spouse<S>3618</S>; a spring<S>1530</S> shut up<S>5274</S>, a fountain<S>4599</S> sealed<S>2856</S>. <sup>inclosed: Heb. barred</sup> <sup>shut up: Heb. barred</sup>

Thy plants<S>7973</S> are an orchard<S>6508</S> of pomegranates<S>7416</S>, with pleasant<S>4022</S> fruits<S>6529</S>; camphire<S>3724</S>, with spikenard<S>5373</S>, <sup>camphire: or, cypress</sup>

Spikenard<S>5373</S> and saffron<S>3750</S>; calamus<S>7070</S> and cinnamon<S>7076</S>, with all trees<S>6086</S> of frankincense<S>3828</S>; myrrh<S>4753</S> and aloes<S>174</S>, with all the chief<S>7218</S> spices<S>1314</S>:
A fountain<S>4599</S> of gardens<S>1588</S>, a well<S>875</S> of living<S>2416</S> waters<S>4325</S>, and streams<S>5140</S> from Lebanon<S>3844</S>.

Awake<S>5782</S>, O north wind<S>6828</S>; and come<S>935</S>, thou south<S>8486</S>; blow<S>6315</S> upon my garden<S>1588</S>, that the spices<S>1314</S> thereof may flow out<S>5140</S>. Let my beloved<S>1730</S> come<S>935</S> into his garden<S>1588</S>, and eat<S>398</S> his pleasant<S>4022</S> fruits<S>6529</S>.

5.text: I am come<S>935</S> into my garden<S>1588</S>, my sister<S>269</S>, my spouse<S>3618</S>: I have gathered<S>717</S> my myrrh<S>4753</S> with my spice<S>1313</S>; I have eaten<S>398</S> my honeycomb<S>3293</S> with my honey<S>1706</S>; I have drunk<S>8354</S> my wine<S>3196</S> with my milk<S>2461</S>: eat<S>398</S>, O friends<S>7453</S>; drink<S>8354</S>, yea, drink abundantly<S>7937</S>, O beloved<S>1730</S>. <sup>yea: or, and be drunken with loves</sup>

I sleep<S>3463</S>, but my heart<S>3820</S> waketh<S>5782</S>: it is the voice<S>6963</S> of my beloved<S>1730</S> that knocketh<S>1849</S>, saying, Open<S>6605</S> to me, my sister<S>269</S>, my love<S>7474</S>, my dove<S>3123</S>, my undefiled<S>8535</S>: for my head<S>7218</S> is filled<S>4390</S> with dew<S>2919</S>, and my locks<S>6977</S> with the drops<S>7447</S> of the night<S>3915</S>.
I have put off<S>6584</S> my coat<S>3801</S>; how<S>349</S> shall I put it on<S>3847</S>? I have washed<S>7364</S> my feet<S>7272</S>; how<S>349</S> shall I defile<S>2936</S> them?

My beloved<S>1730</S> put in<S>7971</S> his hand<S>3027</S> by<S>4480</S> the hole<S>2356</S> of the door, and my bowels<S>4578</S> were moved<S>1993</S> for him. <sup>for him: or, (as some read) in me</sup>

I rose up<S>6965</S> to open<S>6605</S> to my beloved<S>1730</S>; and my hands<S>3027</S> dropped<S>5197</S> with myrrh<S>4753</S>, and my fingers<S>676</S> with sweet smelling<S>5674</S> myrrh<S>4753</S>, upon the handles<S>3709</S> of the lock<S>4514</S>. <sup>sweet: Heb. passing, or, running about</sup>

I opened<S>6605</S> to my beloved<S>1730</S>; but my beloved<S>1730</S> had withdrawn<S>2559</S> himself, and was gone<S>5674</S>: my soul<S>5315</S> failed<S>3318</S> when he spake<S>1696</S>: I sought<S>1245</S> him, but I could not find<S>4672</S> him; I called<S>7121</S> him, but he gave me no answer<S>6030</S>.

The watchmen<S>8104</S> that went about<S>5437</S> the city<S>5892</S> found<S>4672</S> me, they smote<S>5221</S> me, they wounded<S>6481</S> me; the keepers<S>8104</S> of the walls<S>2346</S> took away<S>5375</S> my veil<S>7289</S> from me.

I charge<S>7650</S> you, O daughters<S>1323</S> of Jerusalem<S>3389</S>, if ye find<S>4672</S> my beloved<S>1730</S>, that ye tell<S>5046</S> him, that I am sick<S>2470</S> of love<S>160</S>. <sup>that ye: Heb. what, etc</sup>

What is thy beloved<S>1730</S> more than another beloved<S>1730</S>, O thou fairest<S>3303</S> among women<S>802</S>? what is thy beloved<S>1730</S> more than another beloved<S>1730</S>, that thou<S>3602</S> dost so charge<S>7650</S> us?
My beloved<S>1730</S> is white<S>6703</S> and ruddy<S>122</S>, the chiefest<S>1713</S> among ten thousand<S>7233</S>. <sup>the chiefest: Heb. a standard bearer</sup>
His head<S>7218</S> is as the most<S>3800</S> fine gold<S>6337</S>, his locks<S>6977</S> are bushy<S>8534</S>, and black<S>7838</S> as a raven<S>6158</S>. <sup>bushy: or, curled</sup>

His eyes<S>5869</S> are as the eyes of doves<S>3123</S> by the rivers<S>650</S> of waters<S>4325</S>, washed<S>7364</S> with milk<S>2461</S>, and fitly set<S>3427</S> <S>4402</S>. <sup>fitly: Heb. sitting in fulness, that is, fitly placed, and set as a precious stone in the foil of a ring</sup>

His cheeks<S>3895</S> are as a bed<S>6170</S> of spices<S>1314</S>, as sweet<S>4840</S> flowers<S>4026</S>: his lips<S>8193</S> like lilies<S>7799</S>, dropping<S>5197</S> sweet smelling<S>5674</S> myrrh<S>4753</S>. <sup>sweet flowers: or, towers of perfumes</sup>

His hands<S>3027</S> are as gold<S>2091</S> rings<S>1550</S> set<S>4390</S> with the beryl<S>8658</S>: his belly<S>4578</S> is as bright<S>6247</S> ivory<S>8127</S> overlaid<S>5968</S> with sapphires<S>5601</S>.

His legs<S>7785</S> are as pillars<S>5982</S> of marble<S>8336</S>, set<S>3245</S> upon sockets<S>134</S> of fine gold<S>6337</S>: his countenance<S>4758</S> is as Lebanon<S>3844</S>, excellent<S>977</S> as the cedars<S>730</S>.

His mouth<S>2441</S> is most sweet<S>4477</S>: yea, he is altogether lovely<S>4261</S>. This is my beloved<S>1730</S>, and this is my friend<S>7453</S>, O daughters<S>1323</S> of Jerusalem<S>3389</S>. <sup>mouth: Heb. palate</sup>

6.text: Whither is thy beloved<S>1730</S> gone<S>1980</S>, O thou fairest<S>3303</S> among women<S>802</S>? whither is thy beloved<S>1730</S> turned aside<S>6437</S>? that we may seek<S>1245</S> him with thee.

My beloved<S>1730</S> is gone down<S>3381</S> into his garden<S>1588</S>, to the beds<S>6170</S> of spices<S>1314</S>, to feed<S>7462</S> in the gardens<S>1588</S>, and to gather<S>3950</S> lilies<S>7799</S>.
I am my beloved's<S>1730</S>, and my beloved<S>1730</S> is mine: he feedeth<S>7462</S> among the lilies<S>7799</S>.
Thou art beautiful<S>3303</S>, O my love<S>7474</S>, as Tirzah<S>8656</S>, comely<S>5000</S> as Jerusalem<S>3389</S>, terrible<S>366</S> as an army with banners<S>1713</S>.

Turn away<S>5437</S> thine eyes<S>5869</S> from me, for they<S>1992</S> have overcome<S>7292</S> me: thy hair<S>8181</S> is as a flock<S>5739</S> of goats<S>5795</S> that appear<S>1570</S> from Gilead<S>1568</S>. <sup>overcome: or, puffed me up</sup>

Thy teeth<S>8127</S> are as a flock<S>5739</S> of sheep<S>7353</S> which go up<S>5927</S> from the washing<S>7367</S>, whereof every one beareth twins<S>8382</S>, and there is not one barren<S>7909</S> among them.
As a piece<S>6400</S> of a pomegranate<S>7416</S> are thy temples<S>7541</S> within<S>1157</S> thy locks<S>6777</S>.
There are threescore<S>8346</S> queens<S>4436</S>, and fourscore<S>8084</S> concubines<S>6370</S>, and virgins<S>5959</S> without number<S>4557</S>.

My dove<S>3123</S>, my undefiled<S>8535</S> is but one<S>259</S>; she is the only one<S>259</S> of her mother<S>517</S>, she is the choice<S>1249</S> one of her that bare<S>3205</S> her. The daughters<S>1323</S> saw<S>7200</S> her, and blessed<S>833</S> her; yea, the queens<S>4436</S> and the concubines<S>6370</S>, and they praised<S>1984</S> her.

Who is she that looketh forth<S>8259</S> as the morning<S>7837</S>, fair<S>3303</S> as the moon<S>3842</S>, clear<S>1249</S> as the sun<S>2535</S>, and terrible<S>366</S> as an army with banners<S>1713</S>?

I went down<S>3381</S> into the garden<S>1594</S> of nuts<S>93</S> to see<S>7200</S> the fruits<S>3</S> of the valley<S>5158</S>, and to see<S>7200</S> whether the vine<S>1612</S> flourished<S>6524</S>, and the pomegranates<S>7416</S> budded<S>5132</S>.

Or ever I was aware<S>3045</S>, my soul<S>5315</S> made<S>7760</S> me like the chariots<S>4818</S> of Amminadib<S>5993</S>. <sup>Or ever: Heb. I knew not</sup> <sup>made: or, set me on the chariots of my willing people</sup>

Return<S>7725</S>, return<S>7725</S>, O Shulamite<S>7759</S>; return<S>7725</S>, return<S>7725</S>, that we may look<S>2372</S> upon thee. What will ye see<S>2372</S> in the Shulamite<S>7759</S>? As it were the company<S>4246</S> of two armies<S>4264</S>. <sup>of: or, of Mahanaim</sup>

7.text: How beautiful<S>3302</S> are thy feet<S>6471</S> with shoes<S>5275</S>, O prince's<S>5081</S> daughter<S>1323</S>! the joints<S>2542</S> of thy thighs<S>3409</S> are like jewels<S>2481</S>, the work<S>4639</S> of the hands<S>3027</S> of a cunning workman<S>542</S>.

Thy navel<S>8326</S> is like a round<S>5469</S> goblet<S>101</S>, which wanteth<S>2637</S> not liquor<S>4197</S>: thy belly<S>990</S> is like an heap<S>6194</S> of wheat<S>2406</S> set about<S>5473</S> with lilies<S>7799</S>. <sup>liquor: Heb. mixture</sup>
Thy two<S>8147</S> breasts<S>7699</S> are like two<S>8147</S> young<S>6082</S> roes<S>6646</S> that are twins<S>8380</S>.

Thy neck<S>6677</S> is as a tower<S>4026</S> of ivory<S>8127</S>; thine eyes<S>5869</S> like the fishpools<S>1295</S> in Heshbon<S>2809</S>, by the gate<S>8179</S> of Bathrabbim<S>1337</S>: thy nose<S>639</S> is as the tower<S>4026</S> of Lebanon<S>3844</S> which looketh<S>6822</S> toward<S>6440</S> Damascus<S>1834</S>.

Thine head<S>7218</S> upon thee is like Carmel<S>3760</S>, and the hair<S>1803</S> of thine head<S>7218</S> like purple<S>713</S>; the king<S>4428</S> is held<S>631</S> in the galleries<S>7298</S>. <sup>Carmel: or, crimson</sup> <sup>held: Heb. bound</sup>
How fair<S>3302</S> and how pleasant<S>5276</S> art thou, O love<S>160</S>, for delights<S>8588</S>!
This thy stature<S>6967</S> is like<S>1819</S> to a palm tree<S>8558</S>, and thy breasts<S>7699</S> to clusters<S>811</S> of grapes.

I said<S>559</S>, I will go up<S>5927</S> to the palm tree<S>8558</S>, I will take hold<S>270</S> of the boughs<S>5577</S> thereof: now also thy breasts<S>7699</S> shall be as clusters<S>811</S> of the vine<S>1612</S>, and the smell<S>7381</S> of thy nose<S>639</S> like apples<S>8598</S>;

And the roof of thy mouth<S>2441</S> like the best<S>2896</S> wine<S>3196</S> for my beloved<S>1730</S>, that goeth<S>1980</S> down sweetly<S>4339</S>, causing the lips<S>8193</S> of those that are asleep<S>3463</S> to speak<S>1680</S>. <sup>sweetly: Heb. straightly</sup> <sup>of those: or, of the ancient</sup>
I am my beloved's<S>1730</S>, and his desire<S>8669</S> is toward me.
Come<S>3212</S>, my beloved<S>1730</S>, let us go forth<S>3318</S> into the field<S>7704</S>; let us lodge<S>3885</S> in the villages<S>3723</S>.

Let us get up early<S>7925</S> to the vineyards<S>3754</S>; let us see<S>7200</S> if the vine<S>1612</S> flourish<S>6524</S>, whether the tender grape<S>5563</S> appear<S>6605</S>, and the pomegranates<S>7416</S> bud forth<S>5132</S>: there will I give<S>5414</S> thee my loves<S>1730</S>. <sup>appear: Heb. open</sup>

The mandrakes<S>1736</S> give<S>5414</S> a smell<S>7381</S>, and at our gates<S>6607</S> are all manner of pleasant<S>4022</S> fruits, new<S>2319</S> and old<S>3465</S>, which I have laid up<S>6845</S> for thee, O my beloved<S>1730</S>.

8.text: O that<S>5414</S> thou wert as my brother<S>251</S>, that sucked<S>3243</S> the breasts<S>7699</S> of my mother<S>517</S>! when I should find<S>4672</S> thee without<S>2351</S>, I would kiss<S>5401</S> thee; yea, I should not be despised<S>936</S>. <sup>I should not: Heb. they should not despise me</sup>

I would lead<S>5090</S> thee, and bring<S>935</S> thee into my mother's<S>517</S> house<S>1004</S>, who would instruct<S>3925</S> me: I would cause thee to drink<S>8248</S> of spiced<S>7544</S> wine<S>3196</S> of the juice<S>6071</S> of my pomegranate<S>7416</S>.
His left hand<S>8040</S> should be under my head<S>7218</S>, and his right hand<S>3225</S> should embrace<S>2263</S> me.

I charge<S>7650</S> you, O daughters<S>1323</S> of Jerusalem<S>3389</S>, that ye stir not up<S>5782</S>, nor awake<S>5782</S> my love<S>160</S>, until he please<S>2654</S>. <sup>that: Heb. why should ye stir up, or, why, etc</sup>

Who is this that cometh up<S>5927</S> from the wilderness<S>4057</S>, leaning<S>7514</S> upon her beloved<S>1730</S>? I raised<S>5782</S> thee up under the apple tree<S>8598</S>: there thy mother<S>517</S> brought thee forth<S>2254</S>: there she brought thee forth<S>2254</S> that bare<S>3205</S> thee.

Set<S>7760</S> me as a seal<S>2368</S> upon thine heart<S>3820</S>, as a seal<S>2368</S> upon thine arm<S>2220</S>: for love<S>160</S> is strong<S>5794</S> as death<S>4194</S>; jealousy<S>7068</S> is cruel<S>7186</S> as the grave<S>7585</S>: the coals<S>7565</S> thereof are coals<S>7565</S> of fire<S>784</S>, which hath a most vehement flame<S>7957</S>. <sup>cruel: Heb. hard</sup>

Many<S>7227</S> waters<S>4325</S> cannot<S>3201</S> quench<S>3518</S> love<S>160</S>, neither can the floods<S>5104</S> drown<S>7857</S> it: if a man<S>376</S> would give<S>5414</S> all the substance<S>1952</S> of his house<S>1004</S> for love<S>160</S>, it would utterly<S>936</S> be contemned<S>936</S>.
We have a little<S>6996</S> sister<S>269</S>, and she hath no breasts<S>7699</S>: what shall we do<S>6213</S> for our sister<S>269</S> in the day<S>3117</S> when she shall be spoken for<S>1696</S>?

If she be a wall<S>2346</S>, we will build<S>1129</S> upon her a palace<S>2918</S> of silver<S>3701</S>: and if she be a door<S>1817</S>, we will inclose<S>6696</S> her with boards<S>3871</S> of cedar<S>730</S>.
I am a wall<S>2346</S>, and my breasts<S>7699</S> like towers<S>4026</S>: then was I in his eyes<S>5869</S> as one that found<S>4672</S> favour<S>7965</S>. <sup>favour: Heb. peace</sup>

Solomon<S>8010</S> had a vineyard<S>3754</S> at Baalhamon<S>1174</S>; he let out<S>5414</S> the vineyard<S>3754</S> unto keepers<S>5201</S>; every one<S>376</S> for the fruit<S>6529</S> thereof was to bring<S>935</S> a thousand<S>505</S> pieces of silver<S>3701</S>.

My vineyard<S>3754</S>, which is mine, is before<S>6440</S> me: thou, O Solomon<S>8010</S>, must have a thousand<S>505</S>, and those that keep<S>5201</S> the fruit<S>6529</S> thereof two hundred<S>3967</S>.
Thou that dwellest<S>3427</S> in the gardens<S>1588</S>, the companions<S>2270</S> hearken<S>7181</S> to thy voice<S>6963</S>: cause me to hear<S>8085</S> it.

Make haste<S>1272</S>, my beloved<S>1730</S>, and be thou like<S>1819</S> to a roe<S>6643</S> or to a young<S>6082</S> hart<S>354</S> upon the mountains<S>2022</S> of spices<S>1314</S>. <sup>Make: Heb. Flee away</sup>
